title: The Silmarillion
author: J. R. R. Tolkien
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Silmarillion" book by J. R. R. Tolkien?
Options:
A. In this way he got his name, Bor, whereas his name before had been Balan; for Bor signified 'Vassal' in the tongue of his people. The rule of his folk he committed to Baran his elder son; and he did not return again to Estolad. Soon after the departure of Felagund the other Men of whom Bor had spoken came also into Beleriand. First came the Haladin; but meeting the unfriendship of the Green-elves they turned north and dwelt in Thargelion, in the country of Caranthir son of Fanor; there for a time they had peace, and the people of Caranthir paid little heed to them.
B. In this way he acquired the name Bor, when previously he was known as Balan; since Bor translated to 'Vassal' in the language of his clan. He entrusted the governance of his people to his older son Baran; and did not return to Estolad again. Soon after Felagund's exit, the other Men spoken of by Bor also entered Beleriand. The Haladin came first; but due to the hostility of the Green-elves, they went northward and inhabited Thargelion, in the realm of Caranthir son of Fanor; there they had peace for a time, and the people of Caranthir paid them little mind.
C. In this manner, he acquired the name Bor, whereas previously his name had been Balan; for Bor meant 'Vassal' in the language of his people. He handed over the rule of his people to Baran his older son; and he did not return again to Estolad. Soon after Felagund's departure, the other Men of whom Bor had spoken also entered Beleriand. First came the Haladin; but upon facing the hostility of the Green-elves they went north and lived in Thargelion, in the land of Caranthir son of Fanor; there for a time they had peace, and the people of Caranthir paid little attention to them.
D. That's how he got the name Bor, when before he was called Balan; because Bor meant 'Vassal' in his people's tongue. He left his elder son Baran in charge of leading his folk; and he never went back to Estolad. Not long after Felagund had gone, the other Men that Bor mentioned also came to Beleriand. The Haladin were the first; but because of the unfriendliness of the Green-elves, they turned north and settled in Thargelion, in the land belonging to Caranthir son of Fanor; there they had peace for a while, and the people of Caranthir took little notice of them.
Answer:

The rule of his folk he committed to Baran his elder son; and he did not return again to Estolad. Soon after the departure of Felagund the other Men of whom Bor had spoken came also into Beleriand. First came the Haladin; but meeting the unfriendship of the Green-elves they turned north and dwelt in Thargelion, in the country of Caranthir son of Fanor; there for a time they had peace, and the people of Caranthir paid little heed to them.
